elements.sent-2411-003: This study investigates the structural properties of strange quark stars (SQS) using a Quantum Chromodynamics (QCD) perturbative model combined with the latest Particle Data Group dataset.
elements.sent-2411-004: Given the energy scale present in compact stars, QCD perturbation theory alone may not fully explain their structure.
elements.sent-2411-005: To account for non-perturbative contributions, we incorporate a density-dependent effective bag parameter, $B$, and derive the equation of state (EOS) for strange quark matter (SQM).
elements.sent-2411-006: We start by demonstrating the limitations of EOSs with a constant $B$ in describing massive objects with $ M_{TOV > 2M_{\odot $.}}
elements.sent-2411-007: Subsequently, we show that considering $B$ as a density-dependent function significantly changes the results.
elements.sent-2411-008: Our definition of $B$ includes two parameters determined by both theoretical and observational constraints.
elements.sent-2411-009: We demonstrate that incorporating a density-dependent $B$ into the perturbative EOS can yield SQSs with masses exceeding $2M_{\odot}$, while complying with gravitational wave constraints such as tidal deformability, and thermodynamic considerations, including stability conditions and speed of sound behavior.
elements.sent-2411-010: Specifically, we show that massive compact objects like PSR J0952-0607, PSR J2215+5135, PSR J0740+6620, and the secondary mass of GW190814 can be SQSs.
elements.sent-2411-011: Keywords:strange quark stars; QCD perturbative model; gravitational waves; tidal deformability; equation of state; non-perturbative contributions; thermodynamic stability.
elements.sent-2411-012: Gravitational waves have reshaped our understanding of compact objects such as neutron stars and black holes, offering valuable insights into their origins, evolution, and structure.
elements.sent-2411-013: The neutron star binary mergers detected by LIGO and Virgo have provided crucial information on the masses, spins, and tidal deformabilities ($\Lambda$) of compact stars \cite{Abbott2017,Abbott2020,Abbott2020ApJL .}
elements.sent-2411-014: This data has the potential to revolutionize our understanding of the properties of matter under extreme conditions characterized by high density and pressure, where conventional laboratory experiments are not feasible.
elements.sent-2411-015: Indeed, gravitational wave observations provide valuable constraints on the equation of state (EOS), which describes the relationship between pressure, density, and the structure of matter inside neutron stars.
elements.sent-2411-016: Analysis of GW170817 revealed a tight constraint on the tidal deformability parameter \cite{Abbott2018 , which disfavors very stiff EOSs \cite{IBombaci2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: This feature assists in narrowing down the range of possible EOSs, offering significant constraints for theoretical models aiming to describe the interiors of neutron stars \cite{ITews2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: One of the challenges in investigating the internal structure of compact stars is determining the presence or absence of the quark matter phase.
elements.sent-2411-019: Theoretical Researchers such as Terazawa, Witten, and Bodmer have proposed the existence of quark stars based on the extreme conditions within compact stars \cite{Terazawa1989,Witten1984,ABodmer .}
elements.sent-2411-020: However, obtaining direct observational evidence for quark stars is challenging because of their similarities to neutron stars.
elements.sent-2411-021: Ref. \cite{Annala , through independent model analysis, illustrates that when the conformal bound ($v^2/c^2\leq \frac{1 {3}$, where $v$ is the speed of sound and $c$ is the light speed) is not significantly violated, massive neutron stars are anticipated to contain substantial quark-matter cores.}}
elements.sent-2411-022: As a result, theoretically, compact stars are commonly classified into three categories: i) Neutron stars, which consist entirely of hadronic matter \cite{Wiringa, Stone2007, Pearson, Negreiros,BordbarNS1,BordbarNS2,BordbarNS3,BordbarNS4 , ii) Hybrid stars, characterized by a quark core surrounded by hadronic layers \cite{Blaschke2001, Burgio2003, Alford2005, Pal2023, Rather2023, Li2023,BordbarNSQ1,BordbarNSQ2 , and iii) strange quark stars (SQSs), comprised entirely of strange quark matter (SQM) \cite{Michel1988,Drago2001,Kurkela2010,Wang2019,Deb2021, Sedaghat2021,JSedaghat,BordbarSQS1 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: In this paper, our aim is to investigate the structural properties of SQSs using a Quantum Chromodynamics (QCD) perturbative model along with a density-dependent effective bag constant to account for non-perturbative contributions.
elements.sent-2411-024: To elaborate, our study shows that by modifying the perturbative EOS of SQM with a density-dependent effective bag constant, SQSs can exist with masses exceeding twice that of the Sun (such as PSR J0952-0607 \cite{J0952 , PSR J2215+5135 \cite{Linares2018 , PSR J0740+6620 \cite {Cromartie2019 , and the secondary mass of GW190814 \cite{Abbott2020,ZMiao2021 ), while satisfying gravitational wave constraints like tidal deformability.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Additionally, this approach addresses thermodynamic considerations, including the stability condition of quark matter and speed of sound behavior.
elements.sent-2411-026: To describe SQM, the QCD perturbative model has advantages over other approaches such as the NJL model \cite{Mishustin, Hanauske, Buballa2005, Chu2016, ChengMingLi2020, sedaghat and bag models \cite{Hua Li2010, MassIV, Deb2018, Podder2024 for the following reasons.}}
elements.sent-2411-027: This aspect is essential for accurately describing the EOS of SQM.
elements.sent-2411-028: However, at the energy scale present in compact stars, perturbative QCD may not fully explain their structure.
elements.sent-2411-029: To address this, we introduce an effective bag parameter to incorporate non-perturbative contributions into the thermodynamic potential.
elements.sent-2411-030: Initially, we consider this non-perturbative component as a constant parameter.
elements.sent-2411-031: Subsequently, we define it as a density-dependent function to investigate the structural properties of SQS.
elements.sent-2411-032: The structure of this paper is as follows: In the next section, we examine the running coupling and running strange mass using the latest Particle Data Group dataset.
elements.sent-2411-033: We then introduce a thermodynamic potential that includes both perturbative and non-perturbative components to calculate the EOS of SQM.
elements.sent-2411-034: Next, we treat the non-perturbative component as a constant parameter and determine its permissible values based on the stability condition of SQM.
elements.sent-2411-035: Following this, we obtain the EOSs of SQM and investigate its thermodynamic properties.
elements.sent-2411-036: We then calculate the structural properties of SQS to assess whether the results meet observational constraints.
elements.sent-2411-037: Subsequently, we repeat these steps by defining a density-dependent function for the non-perturbative component of the EOS and investigate its impact on the structural properties of SQS, considering observational constraints from different pulsars and the binaries of GW170817 and GW190814.
elements.sent-2411-038: We conclude the paper with some final remarks.

elements.sent-2411-103: In this section, we explore the structural properties of SQS using the EOSs obtained in the previous section, with a focus on gravitational mass, corresponding radius, and tidal deformability.

elements.sent-2411-106: To obtain the mass in terms of energy density as well as the mass in terms of radius, we employ the TOV equation \cite{Tolman1939,Oppenheimer1939 . \begin{eqnarray}\frac{dP(r)}{dr} = \dfrac{\left[ P(r)+\epsilon (r)\right] \left[ M(r)+4\pi r^{3}P(r)\right] }{r\left( 2M(r)-r\right) },\label{TOV}\end{eqnarray} where, $r$ denotes the radial coordinate of the star.}

elements.sent-2411-142: As we know, with increasing density, the QCD coupling constant decreases, leading to a corresponding decrease in the bag constant.

elements.section-2411-010: Polytropic Models of EOS vs Perturbative QCD EOS
elements.sent-2411-216: In this section, we review the steps to obtain our perturbative EOS, and highlight the differences between our EOS and that of the MIT bag model.
elements.sent-2411-217: Then, we compare our EOS with the EOS of the authors who use a generalized polytropic form with adjustable parameters.
elements.sent-2411-218: Our method for deriving the EOS of SQM is based on perturbative QCD, rather than the MIT Bag model.
elements.sent-2411-219: In the MIT Bag model, quark interactions are simplified as a bag constant, $B$.
elements.sent-2411-220: However, using a perturbative EOS for quark matter that includes a running coupling constant ($\alpha_s$) and a varying strange quark mass is more accurate than that of the simple Bag model.
elements.sent-2411-221: In a purely perturbative calculation, the bag constant, $B$, is typically set to zero.
elements.sent-2411-222: However, in more realistic models, $B$ should not be zero, as pressure can only be defined up to an additive constant that reflects the pressure difference between the physical and perturbative vacuums \cite{Kurkela2010, Sedaghat2021, JSedaghat .}
elements.sent-2411-223: For this reason, we treat the bag constant as a free parameter in our model, allowing us to include non-perturbative effects that are not accounted for in weak coupling expansion.
elements.sent-2411-224: In fact, if QCD interactions between quarks are ignored, the EOS reverts to the MIT Bag model, with B acting as the bag constant \cite{Kurkela2010, Sedaghat2021, JSedaghat .}
elements.sent-2411-225: To calculate the EOS, we made the following physical assumptions: i) charge neutrality; the system must remain electrically neutral locally.
elements.sent-2411-226: Using these conditions and QCD calculations, we derived the EOS and compared them with observational data from LIGO and VIRGO, focusing on parameters such as mass, radius, and tidal deformability.
elements.sent-2411-227: However, some researchers have taken a different approach to determine the EOS.
elements.sent-2411-228: They use polytropic forms of EOS with adjustable parameters.
elements.sent-2411-229: These parameters are tuned so that the resulting EOSs meet the observational constraints.
elements.sent-2411-230: Comparing both methods could provide valuable insights.
elements.sent-2411-231: In certain studies \cite{Maurya2023,Naeem2021,Azam2016,Azam2017 , a generalized polytropic EOS has been used, which depends on three constants $c_1$, $c_2$, and $c_3$, and a polytropic index $n$ as follows, \begin{equation}P=c_1 \ \epsilon^{1+1/n}+c_2 \ \epsilon + c_3\end{equation} By setting $c_1=0$, $c_2=1/3$, and $c_3=-4/3$, this EOS becomes that of the MIT Bag model \cite{Maurya2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-232: Based on the eight conditions described earlier, we consider three EOSs: the MIT Bag model, the perturbative EOS with constant $B$, and the perturbative EOS with density-dependent $B$.
elements.sent-2411-233: Assuming $B=51 \frac{MeV {fm^3}$ in both MIT bag model and perturbative EOS with constant $B$, and using $B_0=51 \frac{MeV {fm^3}$ with $a=0.4$ for perturbative EOS with density dependent $B$, the fitting these EOSs gives the following polynomials, \begin{equation}\label{polytrop1} P = - 2.48\times 10^{-9} \epsilon^3 + 8.50\times 10^{-6} \epsilon^2 + 0.32\epsilon -68.80\end{equation} (\text{MIT Bag Model ) \begin{equation}\label{polytrop2} P = - 12.85\times 10^{-9} \epsilon^3 + 30\times 10^{-6} \epsilon^2 + 0.29\epsilon -68.68\end{equation} (\text{Perturbative EOS with constant $B$}) \begin{equation}\label{polytrop3} P = - 5.11\times 10^{-7} \epsilon^3 + 7.76\times 10^{-4} \epsilon^2 + 0.08\epsilon -51.95.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-234: If we apply Eq. (\ref{poly-eos ) for the MIT Bag model, only the last two terms ($c_3 \ \epsilon + c_4$) significantly contribute to the EOS.}
elements.sent-2411-235: However, when we apply the perturbative calculations and consider a density-dependent $B$, additional terms become important.

elements.sent-2411-243: Our main contribution in this work is the development of a generalized polytropic EOS derived from QCD calculations with a density-dependent \( B \), incorporating all mentioned specified physical conditions, not by adjusting the coefficients appearing in (\ref{poly-eos ).}
elements.sent-2411-244: Additionally, as discussed in previous sections, the EOSs encompass both low and high mass ranges while also satisfying tidal deformability constraints.
elements.sent-2411-245: In this paper, we have conducted a comprehensive study on the SQS using a QCD perturbative model in conjunction with the most recent dataset from the Particle Data Group.
elements.sent-2411-246: Considering the energy scale in compact stars, QCD perturbation theory on its own might be insufficient to fully describe their structure.
elements.sent-2411-247: To include non-perturbative effects, we introduced an effective bag parameter, $B$, and formulate the EOS for SQM.
elements.sent-2411-248: We initially treated $B$ as a constant parameter and later defined it as a density-dependent function, to explore its impact on the structural properties of SQS.
elements.sent-2411-249: Furthermore, We examined the running coupling constant $\alpha_s$ and the running strange quark mass $m_s(Q)$ using the latest Particle Data Group dataset.
elements.sent-2411-250: The thermodynamic potential of SQM was derived, accounting for both non-interacting and perturbative segments, enabling us to calculate various thermodynamic properties such as pressure, energy density, quark number density, speed of sound, and adiabatic index.
elements.sent-2411-251: We first considered a constant $B$ and determined its permissible range based on the stability condition of SQM, ensuring that the energy per baryon at zero pressure is lower than that of the most stable nuclear matter.
elements.sent-2411-252: We then calculated the EOS for different values of $B$, revealing that an increase in $B$ softens the EOS, influencing the maximum gravitational mass and tidal deformability of SQS.
elements.sent-2411-253: The speed of sound and adiabatic index were computed to ensure they meet the causality and dynamical stability conditions, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-254: By solving TOV equations, we derived the mass-radius relationship and tidal deformability of SQS.
elements.sent-2411-255: Our results indicated the limitations of EOSs with a constant $B$ in describing massive objects with $M> 2.03M_{\odot}$.
elements.sent-2411-256: To achieve a more realistic depiction of SQS, we introduced a density-dependent function for $B$.
elements.sent-2411-257: This function, defined by two parameters including $B_0$ and $a$.
elements.sent-2411-258: For each $B_0$, the allowed values of $a$ are determined by the constraint on tidal deformability.
elements.sent-2411-259: Our findings revealed that incorporating a density-dependent $B$ into the perturbative EOS can lead to SQSs with masses over $ 2M_{\odot $, while also meeting gravitational wave requirements such as tidal deformability, and meeting to thermodynamic criteria including stability conditions and speed of sound limits.}
elements.sent-2411-260: This finding is significant as it suggests that SQS could potentially explain observations of massive compact objects, such as PSR J0952-0607, PSR J2215+5135, PSR J0740+6620, and the secondary mass of GW190814.
elements.sent-2411-261: Finally, it is also important to note that some studies have also utilized a density-dependent bag constant to calculate the EOS of SQM.
elements.sent-2411-262: A model for a density-dependent $B$ includes a Gaussian function, as detailed in Refs.
elements.sent-2411-263: These parameters are typically set using experimental data from CERN, specifically from studies on the formation of a quark-gluon plasma in heavy ion collisions \cite{G.F Burgio2002, Bordbar2012 .}
elements.sent-2411-264: However, the medium created in these collisions differs from that in compact stars.
elements.sent-2411-265: The former occurs at low baryon density and high temperature, whereas the stable SQM is expected to form at high baryon density and low temperature.
elements.sent-2411-266: In addition, the parameters $B_{n_B=0}$ and $ B_\infty $ vary widely.
elements.sent-2411-267: For instance, in \cite{G.F Burgio2002 , $ B_{n_B=0 $ ranges from $200 \, \text{MeV/fm ^3$ to $400 \, \text{MeV/fm ^3$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-268: In contrast in \cite{Podder2024 $B_{n_B=0}$ varies from $50 \, \text{MeV/fm ^3$ to $ 100 \, \text{MeV/fm ^3 $ for quark stars and from $ 260 \, \text{MeV/fm ^3 $ to $ 860 \, \text{MeV/fm ^3 $ for hybrid stars.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-269: In \cite{Pal2023 , for stable SQM, the allowed range for $ B_{n_B=0 $ is $ 57 \, \text{MeV/fm ^3 $ to $ 200 \, \text{MeV/fm ^3 $, and $ B_\infty $ varies from $ 25 \, \text{MeV/fm ^3 $ to $ 50 \, \text{MeV/fm ^3 $.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-270: Additionally, according to various sources \cite{G.F Burgio2002, Prasad2004, Bordbar2012, Podder2024 , $ B_\infty $ shows a broad range, from $ 10 \, \text{MeV/fm ^3 $ to $50 \, \text{MeV/fm ^3 $.}}}
elements.sent-2411-271: The advantages of our model compared to previous models are as follows: I) We defined a density-dependent $B$ considering the medium in SQSs.
elements.sent-2411-272: II) Our model for $B$ contains just two parameters, which are set by theoretical and observational constraints of compact stars.
elements.sent-2411-273: III) The allowed range of parameters is considerably narrower than in previous models.
elements.sent-2411-274: IV) We added $B$ as a non-perturbative contribution to the QCD perturbative EOS, allowing the strange quark mass and coupling constant to vary with energy, whereas previous models considered them as constant parameters.
elements.sent-2411-275: V) The behavior of the sound speed in our model is well-behaved, converging to that of a non-interacting relativistic gas at ultra-high densities.
elements.sent-2411-276: VI) Our model for the EOS of SQS matches the observational data for the mass-radius ($M-R$) relation and the tidal deformability-mass ($\Lambda-M$) relation well.
elements.sent-2411-277: Future research should focus on refining the QCD perturbative model and exploring other non-perturbative effects that may influence the properties of SQM.
elements.sent-2411-278: Additionally, obtaining direct observational evidence for the existence of SQS remains a critical challenge.
elements.sent-2411-279: For example, accounting for the color-flavor-locked (CFL) phase can elevate both the mass and stability of quark matter \cite{Tangphati2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-280: Additionally, introducing a net charge within the star can lead to a considerable increase in mass \cite{Pretel2022a,Pretel2022b .}
elements.sent-2411-281: In the context of massive gravity \cite{sedaghat and F(R,T) gravity \cite{Tangphati2022 , the quark stars can achieve masses that are equal to or exceed the secondary mass observed in GW190814.}}
elements.sent-2411-282: Future studies may focus on further corrections to the EOS and the application of modified gravitational theories.
elements.sent-2411-283: In summary, by investigating the quark stars, we can improve our understanding of the EOS for dense matter which has a key role for predicting the structure, stability, and observational signatures of compact objects.
elements.sent-2411-284: In particular, these studies contribute to understanding fundamental questions about the behavior of matter under extreme conditions, including whether SQM is the true ground state of matter.
elements.sent-2411-285: Additionally, observing or constraining the properties of quark stars through gravitational wave signals, pulsar timing, and other astrophysical methods offers a window into high-energy astrophysics and may help confirm or rule out theories of exotic stars.
elements.sent-2411-286: Reviewing references \cite{Annala, JSedaghat, Zhang2022, Banerjee2021, Zhiqiang Miao, I.Bombaci2021 reinforces the value of studying compact stars, particularly quark stars, by highlighting their unique characteristics and the fundamental insights they offer into extreme states of matter.}
elements.sent-2411-287: Here, it can be said that the EOS that we have derived in this paper can be of interest, because it covers both low and high observed masses well, in addition to exhibiting appropriate sound speed behavior.

elements.section-2411-013: Comparative Analysis of the Bag Constant in Perturbative and MIT Bag Models
elements.sent-2411-290: A question that may arise for readers is why the allowed range of $B$ values, which we have used as the non-perturbative part, are smaller than the allowed range used in the bag model.
elements.sent-2411-291: In this appendix, we address this question in detail.
elements.sent-2411-292: Our approach for deriving the EOS of SQM relies on perturbative QCD (see sections \ref{mass and coupling and \ref{e-p ).}}
elements.sent-2411-293: In this model, we treat $B$ as a free parameter to account for non-perturbative effects not captured by a simple weak coupling expansion.
elements.sent-2411-294: Thus, in the perturbative EOS, some interactions are calculated perturbatively, while the non-perturbative effects are incorporated into the effective value of the bag constant, $B$.
elements.sent-2411-295: On the other hand, the MIT Bag model simplifies all quark interactions into a single bag constant.
elements.sent-2411-296: In Section \ref{sc , we outlined the process for deriving the permissible values of the $B$ constant in relation to the stability conditions of SQM.}
elements.sent-2411-297: We established that the allowable range for $B$ is determined by ensuring that the energy per baryon at zero pressure corresponding to the star's surface remains lower than that of the most stable form of nuclear matter.
elements.sent-2411-298: We apply a similar approach for the MIT bag model.
elements.sent-2411-299: Since the mass of the strange quark is considered constant in the bag model, we assume its mass to be $100 MeV$.
elements.sent-2411-300: Figure \ref{SCBag illustrates the range of allowable bag constant values that meet the stability criteria.}
elements.sent-2411-301: It is evident that the permissible range of $B$ in the bag model is significantly wider compared to the perturbative model (see Fig. \ref{stabilitycondition ).}
elements.sent-2411-302: According to perturbative calculations, the maximum allowable value for $B$ is $51 \frac{MeV {fm^3}$, but in the simple MIT bag model, assuming a strange quark mass of $100\ MeV$, this value can increase to $83 \frac{MeV {fm^3}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-303: This difference arises because, in the perturbative model, a portion of the quark interactions is derived from the perturbative expansion of the thermodynamic potential, whereas in the bag model, all the interactions are entirely characterized by the $B$ parameter.
